data.title: US drillers cut oil and gas rigs for first time in three weeks, Baker Hughes says
data.description: U.S. energy firms this week cut the number of oil and natural gas rigs operating for the first time in three weeks, energy services firm Baker Hughes said in its closely followed report on Friday.

data.title: AutoZone's Slide Signals Buying Opportunity — Market Talk
data.description: Since reaching an all-time high in mid-September, AutoZone is off more than 20%, and Oppenheimer thinks it's a good time to buy. Analysts Brian Nagel and Andrew Chasanoff say in a report that investors have been concerned about increased spending and a potential slowdown in sales growth, but those are likely only temporary, according to the analysts. "In our view, recent, stepped-up investment spending, at the chain, is strategic and disciplined in nature, and shares are poised to rebound meaningfully, as expense pressures wane, and signals of sustained, stronger market share dynamics for AZO turn clearer, in coming quarters," the analysts say, reiterating their outperform rating. AutoZone is up 1.6%.(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Rain Raises $250 Million at $1.95 Billion Valuation for Stablecoin Payments Expansion
data.description:
Rain said it has raised $250 million in a new funding round that values the stablecoin payments firm at $1.95 billion, according to co-founder and chief executive officer Farooq Malik. The round was led by ICONIQ, with participation from Sapphire Ventures, Dragonfly, Bessemer, Lightspeed and Galaxy Ventures, and brings Rain's total funding to more than $338 million. The latest financing arrives as investor interest continues to build around infrastructure that could allow stablecoins to be used more seamlessly for everyday payments across borders.

Malik said the fresh capital could be deployed to expand Rain's presence across North and South America, Europe, Asia and Africa, while also supporting deeper engagement with regulators as global rules around stablecoins continue to take shape. He noted that many markets are now moving toward clearer guidelines covering stablecoin infrastructure, blockchain, custody and wallets, and that additional resources could help Rain proactively pursue licenses as part of its ongoing international expansion.

Rain currently enables clients to launch stablecoin-linked cards on the Visa network, allowing users to spend stablecoins at local merchants or withdraw cash at ATMs in more than 150 countries. The company is also working to connect to additional payment systems, including US-based ACH and Europe's SEPA, through partner financial institutions. Malik said Rain continues to invest in expanding partnerships and its geographic footprint, and the firm has already made acquisitions in the past year, including rewards platform Uptop and currency conversion platform Fern.

data.title: Retail Investors Extend US Stock Buying Streak Into January
data.description:
JPMorgan Securities analysts have highlighted sustained strength in US equities as retail investors continue to deploy capital following a standout 2025. An analysis led by Arun Jain points to unusually strong buying activity in the first four trading days of January, ranking as the second-highest level in almost eight months, with daily purchases consistently above the 85th percentile. The data suggest individual investors remain highly engaged, a dynamic that could be supporting market stability during recent pullbacks as fund flows appear to be exerting more influence than traditional valuation metrics.

The renewed demand builds on a banner year for retail traders, who JPMorgan estimates generated gains of more than 20% in 2025, outperforming the S&P 500. Much of that performance was concentrated early in the year, when individuals aggressively bought during three major selloffs, accounting for roughly 75% of their single-stock exposure for the year. Technology stocks featured prominently in those bets, with strong interest in names such as Nvidia and Tesla , before activity in individual stocks cooled from May and ETF buying took on a larger role. Since the start of this year, retail investors have purchased about $10.1 billion of US equities, well above the recent weekly average, indicating conviction has carried into January.

Market participants say retail's influence continues to expand across asset classes. Citadel Securities estimates individuals now account for about 21% of US equity trading volume and roughly 60% of customer activity at the Options Clearing Corp., while options data show persistent call buying across most of the past year. JPMorgan also noted a notable shift into precious metals, with retail investors buying more shares of the SPDR Gold Shares ETF in 2025 than in the prior five years combined, as rising gold prices and geopolitical risks attracted demand. While firms such as Barclays point to record household exposure to equities, strategists caution that the durability of the buy-the-dip approach could depend on how consumer balance sheets evolve, even as retail investors enter the year with capital still available for themes including quantum computing, robotics, and space travel.

data.title: US legal jobs hit new high, labor data shows
data.description:
By David Thomas

There are more people than ever working in the U.S. legal sector, preliminary data released Friday by the U.S. Labor Department showed.

Legal sector employment totaled a record-breaking 1,208,100 jobs last month, according to preliminary seasonally adjusted data released by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. The count includes a range of legal workers at companies, law firms, government agencies and nonprofits, including paralegals and assistants, but the majority are lawyers, according to BLS.

December's historic high follows several record-breaking months for U.S. legal sector employment, though the November and December figures are still subject to revision.

Overall U.S. employment growth slowed more than expected in December amid job losses in the construction, retail and manufacturing sectors, but a decline in the unemployment rate to 4.4% suggested the labor market was not rapidly deteriorating.

Large and midsized U.S. law firms enjoyed a highly profitable 2025 thanks to soaring client demand and ever-higher billing rates, though a contraction could be possible in 2026, according to a report released this week by the Thomson Reuters Institute and Georgetown Law’s Center on Ethics and the Legal Profession. The Thomson Reuters Institute and Reuters share the same parent company.

Both the Great Recession and the pandemic reversed years of gains in legal industry employment, an August 2025 Reuters review of 35 years of BLS data found. It took until 2022 for employment in the sector to rebound to an earlier peak of 1,179,500 jobs recorded in May 2007, when a period of propulsive growth had increased the number of legal jobs by 26.3% since 1990.

data.title: Week Ahead - Jan 12th
data.description:
The second full week of the year will continue to feature key economic releases for major economies and the start of the earnings season for the largest US banks.

The US will release its inflation rate, which has gained scrutiny as stubborn price growth has led to an increasingly divided Federal Reserve.

Other US releases include retail sales, producer prices, home sales, and trade terms. An eventful week for European releases will include GDP updates from the Germany and the UK, in addition to industrial production and trade balance aggregates from the Eurozone.

In China, trade data will update investors on the impact of increasing protectionism from economic partners and slow efforts to ease manufacturing capacity, while credit aggregates will also be featured.

Meanwhile, Japan will post its current account and machinery orders.

Elsewhere, the Bank of Korea will decide on its policy rate, while inflation rates are due from India and Russia.

data.title: Apple CEO Pay Dips as Board Opposes China Audit
data.description:
Apple (AAPL, Financials) said CEO Tim Cook earned total compensation of $74.3 million in 2025, a slight decline from $74.6 million a year earlier, according to a filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. Cook's $3 million base salary has remained unchanged since 2016.

Cook's stock awards include a retirement vesting provision for his long term leadership and development of Apple, according to the filing. Cook is eligible for retirement after almost a decade as CEO.

Other top CEOs received minor pay cuts. General counsel Kate Adams got $27.03 million, retail chief Deirdre O'Brien $27.05 million, and new CFO Kevan Parekh $22.46 million. Former CFO Luca Maestri got $15.48m.

At Apple's 2026 annual shareholder meeting on Feb. 24, investors will vote on five proposals. The board supports four, including director elections and salary approvals, but opposes a China entanglement audit.

The shareholder resolution asks Apple to analyze the risk of relying on Chinese manufacturing amid anticipated 145% U.S. tariff rises and geopolitical concerns. Nearly 20% of Apple's income originates from China.

The board of Apple said that the audit would impose needless limits since the business already publishes worldwide operations and supply-chain management data.

data.title: Treasury investigates some businesses, banks amid Minnesota fraud probe, Bessent says
data.description:
By David Lawder

The U.S. Treasury Department's Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) has notified some money services businesses they are under investigation for alleged fraud as part of a crackdown on federal social benefits abuses in Minnesota, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Friday.

Bessent also told a virtual news conference that some banks in the state involved in money transfer transactions were being audited by the Internal Revenue Service Civil Enforcement Division for alleged money laundering activities.

He declined to identify the banks and money services firms being scrutinized, but said the U.S. government was determined to get to the bottom of alleged social services fraud schemes in Minnesota that he said had diverted billions of dollars intended for social welfare programs. Bessent said some funds may have "potentially been diverted" to the al Shabaab terrorist group in Somalia, but declined to provide specific evidence.

"We have traced where the money went and are examining it," Bessent told reporters after meeting with several financial institutions in the Minneapolis-St. Paul area.

Bessent said FinCEN has issued a geographic order increasing scrutiny on banks and money transmitters in Hennepin and Ramsey counties in Minnesota — covering both Minneapolis and St. Paul. This will require firms to report additional information on funds transferred outside of the U.S., including FinCEN reports on transactions above $3,000.

IRS TO FORM TASK FORCE

The IRS will also form a task force to investigate nonprofit groups on their handling of pandemic-era tax incentive programs, the Treasury chief said.

Bessent described the welfare fraud situation in Minnesota as "out of control" and blamed Democratic Governor Tim Walz for the problems, adding the Treasury was scrutinizing his role.

"It's clear that Governor Walz has been negligent in his fiduciary duties as the chief executive of the state of Minnesota, that this would happen on his watch," Bessent said. "And we are actively pursuing all leads to see the level of involvement, whether it's limited to just negligence and incompetence, or is something more than that."

Walz has referred to the fraud scandal as a crisis and criticized Trump and other Republicans for what he called bad-faith attacks on him.

data.title: 'War Without Mercy' Review: Revolution of Blood and Vengeance
data.description:
By Stephen Brumwell

By December 1780, Maj. Gen. Nathanael Greene of the Continental Army had already spent more than five years fighting for American independence. Greene was no stranger to war's grim realities, but the situation he encountered in South Carolina, when he assumed command of the war's Southern theater, shocked him. Reporting to Samuel Huntington, the president of the Continental Congress, Greene warned that the region risked being laid waste by rival bands of patriots and pro-British loyalists "who pursue each other with as much relentless fury as beasts of prey." As a trusted subordinate of Gen. George Washington, Greene had hitherto experienced a very different kind of conflict: bloody enough, but largely waged by disciplined armies under commanders abiding by a recognized code of military conduct.

While the Revolutionary War's outcome was settled by engagements that conformed to this conventional model — the Battle of Saratoga in 1777 and the siege of Yorktown four years later — these set-piece encounters were atypical, as Mark Edward Lender and James Kirby Martin demonstrate in "War Without Mercy." Of the distinct combats that historians have identified, more than 90% were not primarily fought by formally organized regular troops but instead involved district militias and other irregulars, including Native American warriors.

Localized armed confrontations typically occurred far from the centers of government, beyond the reach of military authorities who might have enforced the concept of jus in bello ("justice in war"). Originating in reaction to the indiscriminate devastation inflicted by Europe's Thirty Years' War (1618-48), by the mid-18th century this doctrine was widely accepted as a means by which civilized opponents could curb extremes of military violence, especially by protecting such noncombatants as civilians, the wounded and prisoners of war.

During the Revolutionary War, Messrs. Lender and Martin emphasize, "local forces were left to fight according to their own rules, which quite often meant no rules." As the strife continued, it generated "uncontrolled contagions of violence" driven by cycles of retribution. What began as a colonial rebellion against British rule, the authors argue, became an existential struggle for survival.

Eschewing a chronological narrative of events, Mr. Lender, a professor emeritus of history at Kean University, and Mr. Martin, who was a professor emeritus of history at the University of Houston until his death in 2024, deliver a robust and focused discussion of their central thesis, supported by case studies that help explain why the war that smoldered and flared beyond the restraining influence of the main armies became so vicious and vindictive.

Although both sides committed atrocities, "War Without Mercy" lays the blame for setting this brutal agenda squarely upon the patriots. Like all revolutionaries bent upon overthrowing an established regime, they needed to establish superiority as swiftly and overwhelmingly as possible. Ascendancy over the outnumbered Tory supporters of the crown was achieved, even before the onset of formal hostilities, through such intimidatory tactics as the painful and humiliating punishment of tarring and feathering. This terrorism, as the authors characterize it, intensified once war was officially declared.

Wary of counterrevolution, the patriots were prepared to maintain dominance by whatever means necessary. The Declaration of Independence legitimized the cause of the rebels but also empowered them to brand loyalists as traitors who could be legally sentenced to death. Confronted by what the authors call "unrelenting brutality," when given the chance — for example, after the British riposted in the South from late 1778 — this beleaguered minority readily retaliated.

Loyalist fury was compounded by what they considered to be an "unnatural" rebellion. In consequence, Messrs. Lender and Martin observe, white loyalists were prepared to override ingrained prejudices and animosities, fighting alongside Native Americans and escaped black slaves.

More generally, the authors suggest, the revolution's ferocity mirrored the pervasive violence of the Colonial era, when scorched-earth strategies against tribal enemies targeted warriors and their families while the enslaved population was only overawed by "brute force."

Washington's intention to wage a humane and gentlemanly war for American independence was subsumed by proliferating mayhem, especially on the remote frontiers, where practices such as scalping were well established. But ruthlessness also surfaced far from the backcountry. New Jersey, located between New York City — which was occupied by the British from the summer of 1776 until the war's end — and Philadelphia, the de facto revolutionary capital, saw more violent clashes than any other state. Here, as elsewhere, the fortunes of local partisans fluctuated in relation to those of the rival field forces. This instability created lawless zones such as the "neutral ground" of Bergen County in northeast New Jersey and the Pine Barrens in the south, where hardened "banditti" flourished. Nominally pro-British, these marauders were not picky about their prey.

The unforgiving nature of the Revolutionary War was underscored by a subsequent exodus of loyalists. An estimated 75,000 — some 2.5% of the population — had no desire to live within an independent republic that held memories of "terror, disillusion, and death," preferring to uproot themselves to Canada and beyond.

As the U.S. prepares to celebrate its semiquincentennial, "War Without Mercy" reminds us that "liberty or death" was more than a popular patriot rallying cry. It was an expression of the stark alternatives faced by thousands of Americans embroiled in a remorseless civil war.

  • Mr. Brumwell is the author of "Turncoat: Benedict Arnold and the Crisis of American Liberty."

data.title: 'The Making of a Permabear' Review: Investing as a Proud Pessimist
data.description:
By James Grant

Buy low, sell high, get rich. Jeremy Grantham's Wall Street career exactly conforms to this charmed sequence, but with what troubles along the way. The reader, at least, will be glad for them. They constitute the breath and the life of "The Making of a Permabear," a memoir written with the financial journalist Edward Chancellor.

Mr. Grantham, a co-founder and longtime investment strategist at the Boston-based money-management firm GMO, can be said to have dug the ruts and potholes on his own bumpy road. He is a nonconformist. Worse, in the context of today's flyaway stock market, he is a principled, value-minded, dogmatic nonconformist. He has nailed his colors to the mast of investment value (don't overpay for stocks and bonds) and to the idea that profit margins and equity valuations never stray far from their respective long-term averages. He lays down the rule that "capital moves toward profits: excess returns attract competition and bad returns drive capital away. Pretty soon you have mean reversion." Who can disagree?

The market can and does so more often than you might suppose. At intervals since the 2007-09 financial crisis, share prices have risen in defiance of the norms of valuation and mean-reversion on which Mr. Grantham has staked his all. At the moment, the values of blue-chip stocks, calculated according to their cyclically adjusted price-earnings ratio, are the second priciest on record (the exception being not 1929 but the 2000 peak of the dot-com bubble). "The long-run prospects for the broad market look as poor as almost any time in history," Mr. Grantham opines.

The tension between what the market is doing and what, by the author's lights, it ought to be doing, will keep investment-minded readers turning these smart and accessible pages. "The market can do anything," Richard Russell, the author of the Dow Theory Letters, was wont to remind his readers. Nobody knows it better than Mr. Grantham.

Born in the U.K. in 1938, the author attended a second-tier boarding school and the non-Oxbridge University of Sheffield (so a chip for each shoulder) before winning admission to the Harvard Business School, class of 1966. He presents himself as a bloody-minded Yorkshireman and as a thrifty Quaker (if, in a restaurant, "I want a drink, I have a glass of the cheapest wine").

Mr. Grantham fills a dozen or so pages with practical thoughts on investing, including Lesson No. 1 (out of a total of seven): "Do not believe the consensus and particularly the bullish propaganda." He relays the startling observation that today the stock market is 20 times more volatile than the underlying, relatively stable, fundamental data, including interest rates and economic growth, can explain. And he describes the sometimes harrowing volatility of GMO's own business history; indeed, the adventures and misadventures of his firm take center stage in this investment saga, as well they might.

The market came close to putting the firm out of business in what proved the final two full years of the dot-com bubble, 1998 and 1999. Recall that the internet had supposedly rewritten the book on investment value and human potentiality. Refusing to believe it, GMO gave wide berth to the high-tech darlings of the day but instead bought such bubble-market castoffs as high-dividend-paying real-estate investment trusts. "We were seen as stuck in the past while the world had changed dramatically," Mr. Grantham recounts. "The word on the street was that we had 'lost our way.'"

His clients started their exodus in 1998. "And then 1999 became a waterfall. They fired us en masse — billions of dollars flowed out the door. . . . In two and a half years, GMO lost 45% of its book of business, which was a catatonic shock." But the 2001-02 crash proved GMO magnificently right, and people and money reversed course back to the formerly shunned Boston calamity howlers. Within five years, the firm was looking after $165 billion compared with the puny $20 billion that its departing investors had left behind them on the way out the door at the bubble-market highs.

"The realization that you can have an idea," the author reflects, "a new idea, or a different idea, and you can be right, and the market, in general, can be wrong, is the single most powerful lesson, I think, that anyone can learn in this business."

Mr. Grantham and his GMO colleagues have, indeed, proven to be founts of new ideas — from an early understanding of the possibilities of index investing to the adoption of quantitative portfolio modeling to the recognition of the predictive power of stock-price momentum. As to the discovery of the "momentum factor," the author states, GMO beat the finance professors to the punch by 20 years.

Mr. Grantham frequently marvels at the dimwittedness of GMO's competition. He is contemptuous of the Wall Street "strategists" who are forever bullish, no matter what the facts might be. He despises the memory of one "typical chinless twit" who, decades ago, in London, insulted him for his temerity in applying for a job that was so obviously beyond his reach. And he heaps scorn on small, marginal businesses that, while appearing to be statistically cheap in the market, are actually "failures, dogs, crap." Reading the toplofty Mr. Grantham, one can almost hear the voice of Shakespeare's Puck — "What fools these mortals be." It would be insufferable except for the author telling such tales on himself as the one in which he absent-mindedly booked three different client meetings in three different cities at the same time and date.

The author quotes, perhaps more than is strictly necessary, from the letters he's issued to his clients and devotes a pair of chapters to climate change, a concern that has become his late-life vocation. For him, "the science" of climate is long since settled. So no collegial nod here to such voices of climatic moderation as Bjorn Lomborg, Matt Ridley or Steven E. Koonin, the author of "Unsettled: What Climate Science Tells Us, What It Doesn't, and Why It Matters" (2021). In 2013, during a demonstration in Washington, D.C., against the building of the Keystone XL pipeline, he was "committed to getting arrested" until cooler heads — namely, his wife's and his lawyer's — decided the issue. "So, I ran for cover on the third police warning" and watched his defiant daughter, who joined him in the protest, being led off in handcuffs.

The climate is ever-changing, but the confounding human propensity to buy high and sell low remains the same. Mr. Grantham has no time for central bankers and most economists, with the exception of John Maynard Keynes. Mr. Grantham quotes the eminent economist's advice to professional investors who, unwisely, would break step with the herd to test new and unconventional ideas. "If he is successful," wrote Keynes in "The General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money" (1936), "that will only confirm the general belief in his rashness; and if in the short run he is unsuccessful, which is very likely, he will not receive much mercy."

Happily for the readers of this book, and for the clients of GMO, the author has ignored the eminent English cynic, and concludes: "You must dare to be independent. Contrarian impulses are usually better. They are always better in major bubbles and busts." So Jeremy Grantham is no "permabear" after all, the provocative title of this fine and essential work notwithstanding. He is a situational bear, a situational bull and, almost despite himself, at age 87, a gruff, perpetual, starry-eyed idealist.

  • Mr. Grant is the founder and editor of Grant's Interest Rate Observer and the author of "Friends Until the End: Edmund Burke and Charles Fox in the Age of Revolution."

data.title: Wall Street jostles to advise on Rio-Glencore, eyes $100 million prize
data.description:
Key points:
  • Wall Street banks compete for advisory roles, eye big fees
  • Rio aims to acquire Glencore, create $200 billion mining giant
  • Previous merger talks between Rio and Glencore failed

By Andres Gonzalez, Amy-Jo Crowley and Anousha Sakoui

Rio Tinto's ambition to acquire Glencore and create the world's largest mining company worth more than $200 billion could see Wall Street advisers share more than $100 million in fees.

Hours after the two mining giants said on Thursday they are discussing merging through an all-share buyout of "some or all" of Glencore by Rio, the race is on to secure a slice of the spoils, said three people with knowledge of the matter.

A representative for Rio , which has until February 5 to make a formal offer for Glencore or walk away under British takeover rules, said it expects to disclose advisers when a deal firms up. Glencore declined to comment.

Companies typically retain several banking advisers to assist them on negotiations, plans for how to structure their businesses and liaising with investors and other stakeholders.

Fees for merger and acquisition advice vary depending on the complexity of the deal and whether advisers provide funding.

But a deal of the size of Rio-Glencore could generate fees that could easily exceed $100 million for the banks involved, two people with knowledge of such transactions said and data on fees on top M&A deals compiled by LSEG showed.

The companies have yet to formally announce advisers, with some roles still being firmed up, sources at advisory firms jostling for positions told Reuters. There were no advisers listed on either company's announcement confirming the talks, which typically include the deal advisers.

JPMorgan, as so-called corporate broker to Rio, is in the lead to advise the world's biggest iron ore miner. UBS is also a broker to Rio, while Glencore does not have a formal broker.

In Britain publicly traded companies retain corporate brokers to steer them through liaising with investors and typically become the bank they turn to for advice on dealmaking.

Meanwhile, Citi retains ties to Glencore having advised it on previous deals including its failed acquisition of Teck in 2023, two people with knowledge of the matter said.

JPMorgan, Citi and UBS declined to comment.

M&A DEALMAKING TICKING UP

Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley led global M&A rankings last year by fees, which rose 19% from 2024, driven by strong deal activity in North America, Dealogic data shows.

The blockbuster mining deal talks come as bigger corporate M&A ticks up, raising the stakes for advisers that miss out.

There were 68 deals for $10 billion or more in 2025, totalling $1.5 trillion and more than double the previous year, LSEG data shows. Looser U.S. regulatory scrutiny is emboldening big companies and encouraging dealmaking.

Meanwhile, a drop in interest rates over the past year has made it easier to finance deals.

Conditions for big deals are unlikely to get much better, said a banker advising on cross-border transactions who is not involved in the Rio-Glencore talks, adding that potential bidders are thinking of striking now as they have become more comfortable with the debate around tariffs.

There is no certainty that Rio's discussions with Glencore will reach a successful outcome and advisory banks may be left with little or no fees if a deal fails to materialize, leaving them with payments of just a few hundred thousand dollars.

The two miners have discussed combining their operations before and in 2014, Rio rejected a Glencore merger offer, saying it was not in the interest of its shareholders.

Merger talks in late 2024 also ended without a deal.

data.title: The Feds Need to Bail Out the Pizza Industry — WSJ
data.description:
By Joe Queenan

In depressing news for traditionalists, Americans are steadily falling out of love with pizza. A deeply troubling report in this paper says that while pizzerias were once the second-most popular type of restaurant in this country, they now rank sixth, lagging behind coffee shops and eateries selling Mexican-style food.

Evidence of the pizza apocalypse is everywhere. Chains are closing, major franchises are considering putting themselves up for sale, and the entire industry seems desperate to find a way to rekindle Americans' passion for a gastronomic and cultural touchstone they once revered every bit as much as Mom, country and apple pie.

And that's going to take money.

This is why the prospect of a federal government bailout of the pizza industry seems so timely and appropriate. Every time the car industry falters, it gets a bailout. When things go south in the soybean fields, farmers get bailouts. Banks get bailouts, airlines get bailouts, and railroads get bailouts. And all sorts of other wounded institutions, including Amtrak, have been able to look to Washington for substantial financial assistance. So why not pizzerias?

This is not to say that the pizza industry is entirely blameless for its imperiled status. Steadily rising prices, calcified and uninspiring menus and even outmoded décor have all contributed to the decline of a dining option that arrived on these shores in the late 1800s, when immigrants from southern Italy began selling pizza on the sidewalks of New York.

Pizza then exploded in popularity after the Second World War when returning servicemen brought home a seemingly universal passion for the tasty comestible. Soon even the humblest burgh in the hinterland could lay claim to a flourishing pizzeria. Or two.

But just as the car industry was blindsided by the arrival of cheaper, more fuel-efficient vehicles from Japan, the pizza industry has been slow to react to the rise of other dramatically different fast-food dining experiences. Everyone from Chipotle to Starbucks has hammered pizza purveyors, and the companies — perhaps feeling betrayed by fickle consumers — do not seem to know how to respond. That's why a federal bailout of the industry is imperative.

Pizza purveyors could start by figuring out a way to make pizza easier to eat. At no time does the industry ever seem to have addressed the fact that pizza slices are really hard for little kids to hold, that the unwieldy slices often buckle and half the cheese ends up splattering onto the kiddies' clothes, thus turning them against pizza forever. This is a design flaw that can no longer be ignored.

And the industry in general seems reluctant to adopt such "artisanal pizzeria" innovations as lavender-flavored pizza, honey-drenched pizza or vegan pizza made with cashew ricotta.

An industry as hidebound as this one is going to need time to retool and figure out ways to address shifting consumer tastes. Just as the American beer industry eventually realized that it might be a good idea to develop products that actually had some taste, the pizza industry may be in need of a full-scale makeover, with entirely new products and more talented chefs. It may even be time for pizzerias to consider experimenting with less obvious ingredients.

No, Americans may not be ready for pizza made with Camembert, Gruyere or Stilton, but some sort of innovation along these lines may be necessary if the industry is to survive. Once-mighty tech companies that refused to accept the rise of the personal computer quickly disappeared. Car manufacturers that continued to churn out hideous gas guzzlers have long since vanished. The pizza industry could easily suffer the same fate.

Not every industry is worthy of a massive federal bailout. The American people owe nothing to companies that supply fast lube jobs. Nor need they feel beholden to cupcake makers, cobblers, tax preparers or manufacturers of cheap lingerie.

Pizzerias are different. Pizzerias are genuinely iconic retail establishments that define what it means to be an American. For the American people to turn their back on them would be the unkindest cut of all.

data.title: Wilbur Smith, LLC Expands Criminal Defense Practice
data.description:
ATTORNEY LEONARD S. FEUER NAMED INDEPENDENT DIRECTOR WILBUR SMITH CRIMINAL DEFENSE

ATTORNEY MATTHEW R. DePIETRO JOINS WILBUR SMITH CRIMINAL DEFENSE

FORT MYERS, FL / ACCESS Newswire / January 9, 2026 / Said Wilbur C. Smith, III, founder and namesake of Wilbur Smith, LLC, "Protecting our community is the basis and focus of how I founded this Law Firm in 1970. My trust and confidence in Mr. Feuer and Mr. DePietro as members of our Firm is marrow deep."

Leonard S. Feuer, Owner and Managing Partner of the Feuer Law Firm, West Palm Beach, Fla., has represented those in need for thirty years. Mr. Feuer's practice focuses on complex Civil and Criminal Defense, Appellate work, and Complex Litigation. Mr. Feuer adds to Wilbur Smith, LLC, another layer of extraordinary talent, ensuring its clients experience the highest level of representation.

Working with Mr. Feuer, Matthew R. DePietro, adds to the depth chart of Wilbur Smith Criminal Defense. Mr. DePietro is dedicated to ensuring our community, and those within it, are just, enriched, and whole - upholding the mission statement of the Firm. Mr. DePietro is an embodiment of what it means to think faster, work harder, and respond quicker.

Said Sawyer C. Smith, Managing Partner of Wilbur Smith, LLC, "Adding legal talent such as Mr. Feuer and Mr. DePietro, as well as their incredible work ethic, will serve not only our business today but also tomorrow. Welcome aboard, Gentlemen."

Under the leadership of Messrs. Wilbur and Sawyer Charles Smith, the unrelenting dedication to the community and providing the highest level of legal services, continues.

##

CONTACT: Madison Vogelbach

Fort Myers, Fla. (January 9, 2026) (239) 334-7696 | madison@wilbursmith.law

SOURCE: Wilbur Smith Attorneys at Law

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Toronto Stocks Touch New High, Buoyed by Mining and Financial Shares
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

Shares on Canada's main exchange continued to push higher, lifting the benchmark index to a fresh high as a softer employment report did little to dampen investor spirits.

In midday trading, the Toronto Stock Exchange's S&P/TSX Composite Index was 0.9% higher at 32655.39. That has it on track to finish the first full trading week of the year with a gain of 2.4%.

Mining and finance stocks led broad gains, more than making up for weakness in the transportation and commercial services sectors.

The blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 was ahead 0.7% at 1910.14.

With gold and silver prices up for the week so far, and oil prices advancing for a second straight session, resources stocks firmed broadly. Agnico Eagle gained 1.9% to $190.00 and First Quantu Minerals was up 4.5% to $28.59, while Canadian Natural Resources rose 1.3% to $31.96 and Suncor Energy advanced 1.9% to $46.61.

Most of the major banks were higher, led by a 1% increase for National Bank of Canada.

Other market movers:

Aritzia's shares rose 3.6% to C$129.78 on the back of record quarterly revenue for the clothing designer and a lift in its sales target for the fiscal year following a strong holiday season.

Ag Growth International's shares rallied 25% to C$29.66 after the company logged stronger than expected earnings thanks to ongoing international commercial demand and a slightly firmer order book heading into the final quarter of last year.

Algoma Steel retreated 0.7% to C$5.86 after the steelmaker forecast sharply lower steel shipments and a widened loss in the final quarter of last year as the Trump administration's tariffs continued to bite.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com
